schools.summary: Jethro hearing of God's deliverance of Israel: 'when Jethro, the priest of Midian, Moses' father in law, heard of all that God had done for Moses, and for Israel his people, and that the LORD had brought Israel out of Egypt' — Jethro (Reuel, Exodus 2:18, the priest or prince of Midian, Moses' father-in-law) hearing of the great deliverance (the plagues, the Red Sea, the wonders, the report spread abroad, the fame of God's works, Exodus 15:14; Joshua 2:10, the occasion of his visit); the things God had done (for Moses and Israel) — the great works of God (the fame of the deliverance reaching Midian, the report that drew Jethro); 'the LORD had brought Israel out of Egypt' (the great deliverance, the Exodus, the chief of God's works, the report that moved Jethro to come, vv.5, the providence, the fame of God's redemption drawing the Gentile priest) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: When Jethro, the priest of Midian, Moses' father in law, heard of all that God had done for Moses, and for Israel his people, and that the LORD had brought Israel out of Egypt
Jethro hearing of God's deliverance of Israel
the fame of the redemption spreading abroad (Exodus 15:14; Joshua 2:10)
the great deliverance drawing the Gentile priest to come

schools.summary: Jethro bringing Zipporah, Moses' wife: 'then Jethro, Moses' father in law, took Zipporah, Moses' wife, after he had sent her back' — Jethro bringing Zipporah (Moses' wife) and her sons (vv.3-4) to Moses, Zipporah having been 'sent back' earlier (perhaps after the incident of the circumcision, Exodus 4:24-26, or sent home for safety during the Egyptian conflict, the reunion of Moses' family, the bringing of the wife and sons); Zipporah (Moses' wife, Exodus 2:21) — brought by her father to Moses (the reunion); 'after he had sent her back' (Zipporah sent home earlier, to Midian, for safety or other cause, Exodus 4:24-26, now restored to Moses, the providence, the family reunited at the mount of God, vv.5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Then Jethro, Moses' father in law, took Zipporah, Moses' wife, after he had sent her back
Jethro bringing Zipporah, Moses' wife
Zipporah sent back earlier (Exodus 4:24-26), now restored to Moses
the family reunited at the mount (vv.5)

schools.summary: Gershom, the son named for Moses' exile: 'and her two sons; of which the name of the one was Gershom; for he said, I have been an alien in a strange land' — Jethro bringing Moses' two sons, the first, Gershom ('a stranger there'), named because Moses had been an alien (a stranger, sojourner) in a strange land (Midian, Exodus 2:22, the name of the exile, the memorial of Moses' pilgrim state); Gershom (Moses' firstborn, Exodus 2:22) — the son of the exile (the name recalling Moses' sojourning); 'I have been an alien in a strange land' (Moses owning himself a stranger, in Midian, the land of his exile, the pilgrim spirit, the godly strangers on earth, Hebrews 11:13-16, the name a testimony to Moses' faith, looking beyond the exile to the promise) — Wesley, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And her two sons; of which the name of the one was Gershom; for he said, I have been an alien in a strange land
Gershom, the son named for Moses' exile
the name of the exile (Exodus 2:22)
the pilgrim spirit of the godly (Hebrews 11:13-16)

schools.summary: Eliezer, the son named for God's help: 'and the name of the other was Eliezer; for the God of my father, said he, was mine help, and delivered me from the sword of Pharaoh' — Moses' second son, Eliezer ('my God is help'), named because the God of his father had been his help and delivered him from Pharaoh's sword (when Pharaoh sought to slay him, Exodus 2:15, the name of thanksgiving, commemorating God's deliverance, the testimony to God's help); the God of Moses' father (the covenant God) the help and deliverer — the thanksgiving (the faith owning God's deliverance); 'delivered me from the sword of Pharaoh' (God's deliverance of Moses, from Pharaoh's wrath, Exodus 2:15, the name a memorial of the rescue, the faith, the godly naming their children for God's mercies, the providence remembered) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And the name of the other was Eliezer; for the God of my father, said he, was mine help, and delivered me from the sword of Pharaoh
Eliezer, the son named for God's help
the name commemorating God's deliverance from Pharaoh's sword (Exodus 2:15)
the faith owning God's mercy, the godly naming children for his mercies

schools.summary: Jethro coming to Moses at the mount of God: 'and Jethro, Moses' father in law, came with his sons and his wife unto Moses into the wilderness, where he encamped at the mount of God' — Jethro coming with Moses' wife and sons to Moses in the wilderness, where Israel was encamped at the mount of God (Horeb/Sinai, Exodus 3:1; 19:1-2, the visit, the Gentile priest coming to the camp, the reunion of the family, the place, the mount of God, where the law would soon be given); Jethro bringing the family (the wife and sons) — the reunion (the visit); 'where he encamped at the mount of God' (Israel at the mount of God, Horeb/Sinai, the place of the burning bush, Exodus 3:1, and soon of the law, Exodus 19, the providence, the camp at the holy mount, the setting for Jethro's counsel, vv.13-26, before the giving of the law) — JFB, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Jethro, Moses' father in law, came with his sons and his wife unto Moses into the wilderness, where he encamped at the mount of God
Jethro coming to Moses at the mount of God
the Gentile priest to the camp, the family reunited (Exodus 3:1)
the mount of God, soon to be the place of the law (Exodus 19:1-2)

schools.summary: Jethro announcing his coming to Moses: 'and he said unto Moses, I thy father in law Jethro am come unto thee, and thy wife, and her two sons with her' — Jethro sending word (or announcing) to Moses that he had come, with Moses' wife and two sons (the message, perhaps sent ahead, that Moses might come out to meet them, v.7, the announcement of the visit, the bringing of the family); Jethro's announcement (sending word of his coming) — the courteous notice (the message ahead of the meeting); 'thy wife, and her two sons with her' (the wife and sons brought, the reunion, the family restored to Moses, the providence, the family reunited before the meeting, v.7) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And he said unto Moses, I thy father in law Jethro am come unto thee, and thy wife, and her two sons with her
Jethro announcing his coming to Moses
the courteous notice of the visit (v.7)
the family restored to Moses before the meeting

schools.summary: Moses' respectful welcome of Jethro: 'and Moses went out to meet his father in law, and did obeisance, and kissed him; and they asked each other of their welfare; and they came into the tent' — Moses, the great leader of Israel, going out to meet Jethro, bowing (doing obeisance), and kissing him (the respect and affection, Moses honoring his father-in-law, despite his own high station, the courtesy, the meekness of Moses, honoring the aged and the kindred, Exodus 20:12); Moses' obeisance and kiss (the respectful, affectionate greeting) — the humility of the great leader (the honor to the father-in-law, the meekness of Moses, Numbers 12:3); 'they asked each other of their welfare; and they came into the tent' (the friendly inquiry, after each other's welfare, and the going into the tent, the hospitality, the fellowship, the warm reception, the providence, the meeting that would lead to Jethro's worship, v.12, and counsel, vv.13-26) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And Moses went out to meet his father in law, and did obeisance, and kissed him; and they asked each other of their welfare; and they came into the tent
Moses' respectful welcome of Jethro
the meekness of the great leader honoring his father-in-law (Exodus 20:12; Numbers 12:3)
the warm fellowship and hospitality

schools.summary: Moses recounting God's deliverance to Jethro: 'and Moses told his father in law all that the LORD had done unto Pharaoh and to the Egyptians for Israel's sake, and all the travail that had come upon them by the way, and how the LORD delivered them' — Moses recounting to Jethro all the LORD had done (the plagues, the Red Sea, the wonders), the hardships (travail) of the way, and the deliverances (the testimony to God's works, Moses ascribing all to the LORD, the rehearsal of the redemption); Moses recounting God's works (the deliverance, the judgments) — the testimony (the glory given to God); 'all the travail that had come upon them by the way, and how the LORD delivered them' (the hardships, the trials of the wilderness, and the deliverances, God's repeated help, the testimony to God's faithfulness, the providence, the rehearsal that moved Jethro to rejoice and bless God, vv.9-11) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And Moses told his father in law all that the LORD had done unto Pharaoh and to the Egyptians for Israel's sake... and how the LORD delivered them
Moses recounting God's deliverance to Jethro
the testimony to God's works, the glory given to God
the rehearsal that moved Jethro to rejoice and bless God (vv.9-11)

schools.summary: Jethro rejoicing in God's goodness to Israel: 'and Jethro rejoiced for all the goodness which the LORD had done to Israel, whom he had delivered out of the hand of the Egyptians' — Jethro rejoicing (being glad) at hearing of all God's goodness to Israel (the deliverance, vv.8, the joy of the Gentile priest, rejoicing in God's mercy to his people, the gladness at God's works, the response of faith); Jethro's joy (rejoicing in God's goodness, not envying) — the godly gladness (the sharing in the joy of God's people, Romans 12:15, rejoicing with them that rejoice); 'whom he had delivered out of the hand of the Egyptians' (the deliverance, the cause of the joy, Jethro rejoicing in the redemption, the providence, the Gentile priest glad at God's mercy, a foreshadowing of the Gentiles rejoicing in God's salvation, Romans 15:10) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Jethro rejoiced for all the goodness which the LORD had done to Israel, whom he had delivered out of the hand of the Egyptians
Jethro rejoicing in God's goodness to Israel
the godly gladness, rejoicing with them that rejoice (Romans 12:15)
the Gentile priest glad at God's mercy, a foreshadowing of the Gentiles' joy (Romans 15:10)

schools.summary: Jethro blessing the LORD for the deliverance: 'and Jethro said, Blessed be the LORD, who hath delivered you out of the hand of the Egyptians, and out of the hand of Pharaoh, who hath delivered the people from under the hand of the Egyptians' — Jethro blessing (praising) the LORD for delivering Israel from the Egyptians and Pharaoh (the worship of the Gentile priest, ascribing the glory to the LORD, the blessing of God for the redemption, the response of faith); Jethro's blessing of the LORD (the doxology, the praise of God for the deliverance) — the worship (the glory given to God); 'who hath delivered the people from under the hand of the Egyptians' (the deliverance praised, the redemption of Israel, the providence, the Gentile priest blessing God, a foreshadowing of the nations praising God for his salvation, Psalm 117:1; Romans 15:11) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And Jethro said, Blessed be the LORD, who hath delivered you out of the hand of the Egyptians, and out of the hand of Pharaoh
Jethro blessing the LORD for the deliverance
the worship of the Gentile priest, the glory given to God
a foreshadowing of the nations praising God for his salvation (Psalm 117:1; Romans 15:11)

schools.summary: Jethro's confession that the LORD is greater than all gods: 'now I know that the LORD is greater than all gods: for in the thing wherein they dealt proudly he was above them' — Jethro's words — 'Now I know that the LORD is greater than all gods': Jethro confessing, on hearing of the deliverance, that the LORD (Jehovah) is greater than all gods (the gods of Egypt, of the nations, the confession of the Gentile priest, owning the supremacy of the true God, perhaps a fuller conversion, or a confirmation of faith, the witnesses noting the confession — the LORD above all the gods, the false gods of Egypt judged, Exodus 12:12, the incomparable God, Exodus 15:11, 'who is like unto thee, O LORD, among the gods?', the knowledge of God by his works, Jethro learning, by the deliverance, the supremacy of the LORD, a foreshadowing of the Gentiles confessing the true God, the nations coming to the light, Isaiah 60:3, the firstfruits of the Gentile confession); the ground of the confession — 'in the thing wherein they dealt proudly he was above them': in the very matter where the Egyptians dealt proudly (the oppression, the drowning of the infants, the pursuit), God being above them (overruling, judging, the proud drowned in the sea they thought to use, Exodus 14:27-28, the just retribution, God paying the proud in their own coin, the Egyptians drowned as they drowned the infants, the proof of God's supremacy, the proud humbled, the LORD exalted); Jethro's 'now I know' — the knowledge of God's supremacy by the deliverance (the confession of faith, the providence, the works of God teaching the knowledge of the LORD, even to the Gentile) — Wesley, JFB, Gill.

sides.position: The Gentile priest owning the supremacy of the true God, learned by the deliverance (Exodus 12:12; 15:11) - the false gods of Egypt judged, the just retribution where the proud were humbled in their own pride (Exodus 14:27-28); a foreshadowing of the Gentiles confessing the true God (Isaiah 60:3)

themes: Now I know that the LORD is greater than all gods: for in the thing wherein they dealt proudly he was above them
Jethro's confession that the LORD is greater than all gods
the Gentile priest owning the supremacy of the true God (Exodus 12:12; 15:11)
the just retribution where the proud were humbled in their own pride (Exodus 14:27-28); a foreshadowing of the Gentiles' confession (Isaiah 60:3)

schools.summary: Jethro's sacrifice and the fellowship meal: 'and Jethro, Moses' father in law, took a burnt offering and sacrifices for God: and Aaron came, and all the elders of Israel, to eat bread with Moses' father in law before God' — Jethro offering a burnt offering and sacrifices to God (an act of worship and thanksgiving for the deliverance, the worship of the Gentile priest, offering to the true God, the sacrifice of praise, the confession in act, the worship following the confession, v.11); Jethro's offering (the burnt offering, the sacrifices) — the worship of God (the thanksgiving for the deliverance, the priest of Midian offering to Jehovah); 'Aaron came, and all the elders of Israel, to eat bread with Moses' father in law before God' (the fellowship meal, Aaron and the elders eating with Jethro, the sacrificial feast, before God, the communion, the people joined in worship and fellowship with the Gentile priest, the providence, the fellowship a foreshadowing of the Gentiles joined to Israel in the worship of God, the one table, Ephesians 2:11-19, a type of the gospel feast, Jew and Gentile together) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Jethro, Moses' father in law, took a burnt offering and sacrifices for God: and Aaron came, and all the elders of Israel, to eat bread with Moses' father in law before God
Jethro's sacrifice and the fellowship meal
the worship of the Gentile priest, the sacrifice of praise
the fellowship of Jew and Gentile, a foreshadowing of the gospel feast (Ephesians 2:11-19)

schools.summary: Moses judging the people alone all day: 'and it came to pass on the morrow, that Moses sat to judge the people: and the people stood by Moses from the morning unto the evening' — Moses sitting as judge over the people, hearing their causes and disputes, the people standing before him from morning to evening (the great burden of the cases, the sole judge, Moses alone bearing the whole weight, vv.14, the toil of the office); the great burden (the people waiting all day, the cases endless) — the overload (Moses alone unable to bear it, v.18, the toil of the sole judge); Moses judging (settling disputes, teaching the statutes, vv.15-16) — the labor of governing the people (the sole burden, that Jethro would counsel him to share, vv.17-23, the providence, the occasion of the appointing of judges, the ordering of the government) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And it came to pass on the morrow, that Moses sat to judge the people: and the people stood by Moses from the morning unto the evening
Moses judging the people alone all day
the great burden on Moses alone (vv.14, 18)
the occasion of Jethro's counsel and the appointing of judges (vv.17-23)

schools.summary: Jethro questioning Moses' burden: 'and when Moses' father in law saw all that he did to the people, he said, What is this thing that thou doest to the people? why sittest thou thyself alone, and all the people stand by thee from morning unto even?' — Jethro, observing Moses judging alone all day, questioning the arrangement (the wise observation, seeing the burden on Moses, and the people's waiting, the concern, the toil unsustainable, the prelude to the counsel, vv.17-23); Jethro's question (why Moses alone, the burden too great) — the wisdom of the observation (the concern for Moses and the people); 'all the people stand by thee from morning unto even' (the people waiting all day, the cases endless, the delay, the toil, on Moses and the people, the providence, the observation that led to the counsel, the appointing of judges, vv.21-23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And when Moses' father in law saw all that he did to the people, he said, What is this thing that thou doest to the people? why sittest thou thyself alone...
Jethro questioning Moses' burden
the wise observation of the unsustainable toil
the prelude to the counsel and the appointing of judges (vv.17-23)

schools.summary: Moses explaining that the people seek God's will through him: 'and Moses said unto his father in law, Because the people come unto me to enquire of God' — Moses explaining that the people came to him to enquire of God (to know God's will, to have their causes decided by God's law and judgment, the office, Moses the mediator, the people seeking God's mind through him, the reason for the throng, the people's resort to God through Moses); the people coming (to Moses, as God's representative) — the resort to the mediator (the seeking of God's will); 'to enquire of God' (the people seeking God's will, through Moses, the mediator, the prophet, the judging by God's law, the causes decided by the divine statutes, v.16, the providence, Moses the channel of God's will, a type of Christ the Mediator, through whom we enquire of God, Hebrews 7:25) — Wesley, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Moses said unto his father in law, Because the people come unto me to enquire of God
Moses explaining that the people seek God's will through him
Moses the mediator, the channel of God's will
a type of Christ the Mediator, through whom we enquire of God (Hebrews 7:25)

schools.summary: Moses judging and teaching God's statutes: 'when they have a matter, they come unto me; and I judge between one and another, and I do make them know the statutes of God, and his laws' — Moses describing his work: judging between disputants (settling causes) and teaching the people the statutes and laws of God (the double office, judge and teacher, the labor, the whole burden of justice and instruction on Moses, the ground of the throng); Moses judging disputes (deciding causes by God's law) — the office of the judge (the settling of differences); 'I do make them know the statutes of God, and his laws' (Moses teaching God's statutes, instructing the people in the law, the office of the teacher, the people taught God's will, the providence, the double burden, judging and teaching, that Jethro would counsel him to share, vv.21-22) — Wesley, Gill.

themes: When they have a matter, they come unto me; and I judge between one and another, and I do make them know the statutes of God, and his laws
Moses judging and teaching God's statutes
the double office of judge and teacher
the whole burden, that Jethro would counsel him to share (vv.21-22)

schools.summary: Jethro's frank counsel that the burden was not good: 'and Moses' father in law said unto him, The thing that thou doest is not good' — Jethro frankly telling Moses that the arrangement (Moses judging alone) was 'not good' (not wise, not sustainable, the candid counsel, the father-in-law's wise concern, the rebuke of the impractical method, the burden too great, vv.18, the prelude to the better plan, vv.19-23); Jethro's frankness (telling Moses plainly the method was faulty) — the wise, loving candor (the counsel of a friend, faithful are the wounds of a friend, Proverbs 27:6); Jethro's word ('not good') — the wisdom of the counsel (the burden unsustainable, vv.18, Moses' meekness in receiving it, v.24, the providence, the wise counsel, even from a Gentile, accepted by Moses, the ordering of the government) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Moses' father in law said unto him, The thing that thou doest is not good
Jethro's frank counsel that the burden was not good
the faithful, loving candor of a friend (Proverbs 27:6)
the prelude to the better plan (vv.19-23)

schools.summary: The burden too heavy for Moses alone: 'thou wilt surely wear away, both thou, and this people that is with thee: for this thing is too heavy for thee; thou art not able to perform it thyself alone' — Jethro warning that Moses (and the people) would 'wear away' (be worn out, exhausted) by the burden (the toil unsustainable, too heavy for one man, the people too, wearied by the delay, the impossibility of the sole office, Moses unable to bear it alone, Numbers 11:14, 'I am not able to bear all this people alone'); the wearing-out (of Moses and the people, by the burden) — the toil unsustainable (the need of help); 'this thing is too heavy for thee; thou art not able to perform it thyself alone' (the burden beyond one man, Numbers 11:14, the need of the shared office, vv.21-22, the lesson, the wisdom of sharing the load, the delegation of labor, no one man able to do all) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: Thou wilt surely wear away, both thou, and this people that is with thee: for this thing is too heavy for thee; thou art not able to perform it thyself alone
the burden too heavy for Moses alone
the toil unsustainable, beyond one man (Numbers 11:14)
the need of the shared office and delegation of labor (vv.21-22)

schools.summary: Jethro's counsel for Moses to be the people's representative to God: 'hearken now unto my voice, I will give thee counsel, and God shall be with thee: Be thou for the people to God-ward, that thou mayest bring the causes unto God' — Jethro offering his counsel, with the modest qualification 'and God shall be with thee' (so far as God approve, the counsel subject to God's will, v.23, the wise, humble advice, the deference to God, the counsel not against God's mind, but for the ordering of the government); Moses to 'be for the people to God-ward' (the people's representative before God, the mediator) — Moses to retain the great office (the mediator, bringing the people's causes to God, the chief work, the mediation, kept by Moses); 'that thou mayest bring the causes unto God' (Moses to bring the people's causes to God, the hard cases, the great matters, v.22, 26, the mediator's office retained, Moses the channel to God, the providence, Moses keeping the mediation, the lesser cases shared, vv.21-22, a type of Christ the Mediator, bringing his people's causes to God, Hebrews 7:25) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Hearken now unto my voice, I will give thee counsel, and God shall be with thee: Be thou for the people to God-ward, that thou mayest bring the causes unto God
Jethro's counsel for Moses to be the people's representative to God
Moses keeping the great office of mediation, bringing the causes to God
a type of Christ the Mediator (Hebrews 7:25)

schools.summary: Moses to teach the people God's ways: 'and thou shalt teach them ordinances and laws, and shalt shew them the way wherein they must walk, and the work that they must do' — Jethro counseling that Moses should teach the people God's ordinances and laws, showing them the way to walk and the work to do (the office of the teacher, Moses to instruct the people in God's will, the chief work, the teaching and mediation kept by Moses, the lesser cases shared, the instruction of the people); Moses teaching God's ordinances and laws (instructing the people) — the office of the teacher (the people taught God's will); 'shalt shew them the way wherein they must walk, and the work that they must do' (Moses showing the people the way and the work, the practical instruction, how to live and what to do, the guidance in God's ways, Psalm 32:8; Micah 6:8, the providence, Moses the teacher of the people, a type of Christ, the teacher and way, John 14:6) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt teach them ordinances and laws, and shalt shew them the way wherein they must walk, and the work that they must do
Moses to teach the people God's ways
the office of the teacher, instructing the people in God's will
showing the way to walk (Micah 6:8), a type of Christ the teacher and way (John 14:6)

schools.summary: The qualifications of the rulers: 'moreover thou shalt provide out of all the people able men, such as fear God, men of truth, hating covetousness; and place such over them, to be rulers of thousands, and rulers of hundreds, rulers of fifties, and rulers of tens' — the four marks — 'able men, such as fear God, men of truth, hating covetousness': Jethro counseling Moses to choose, for rulers and judges, men marked by four qualifications: (1) 'able men' (men of ability, capacity, courage, wisdom, fit for the office); (2) 'such as fear God' (the fear of God the root of righteous judgment, men who reverence God and so will judge justly, 2 Samuel 23:3, 'he that ruleth over men must be just, ruling in the fear of God'); (3) 'men of truth' (honest, faithful, truthful men, not liars or flatterers); (4) 'hating covetousness' (hating bribes and unjust gain, not to be bought, Deuteronomy 16:19, 'a gift doth blind the eyes of the wise'); the witnesses noting these as the abiding qualifications of all who rule and judge (in church and state) — ability, piety, integrity, and freedom from greed, the model for the choice of rulers, elders, and officers (the like qualifications, Deuteronomy 1:13; Acts 6:3, 'men of honest report, full of the Holy Ghost and wisdom'; 1 Timothy 3:1-7; Titus 1:5-9, the elder not greedy of filthy lucre, the standard for godly government); the graded order (rulers of thousands, hundreds, fifties, tens — a hierarchy of judges, the cases rising from the lower to the higher) — the orderly government (the delegation, the shared office, the wisdom of the arrangement, the lesser cases to the lower, the harder to Moses, v.22) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill, Ellicott.

debates.question: What are the qualifications of the godly ruler and judge?

sides.position: Four marks - able men (capacity, wisdom), such as fear God (the root of righteous judgment, 2 Samuel 23:3), men of truth (honest, faithful), hating covetousness (not to be bribed, Deuteronomy 16:19); the abiding qualifications for officers in church and state (Deuteronomy 1:13; Acts 6:3; 1 Timothy 3:1-7)

themes: Moreover thou shalt provide out of all the people able men, such as fear God, men of truth, hating covetousness; and place such over them, to be rulers of thousands...
the qualifications of the rulers (the crux of the godly judge)
four marks - able, fearing God, men of truth, hating covetousness (2 Samuel 23:3; Deuteronomy 16:19)
the abiding model for the choice of officers in church and state (Deuteronomy 1:13; Acts 6:3; 1 Timothy 3:1-7)

schools.summary: The sharing of the burden among the judges: 'and let them judge the people at all seasons: and it shall be, that every great matter they shall bring unto thee, but every small matter they shall judge: so shall it be easier for thyself, and they shall bear the burden with thee' — the appointed judges to judge the people at all times, deciding the small matters themselves and bringing the great matters to Moses (the shared burden, the lesser cases to the judges, the harder to Moses, the relief of Moses, the load lightened, the orderly government); the division (small cases to the judges, great to Moses) — the orderly arrangement (the graded jurisdiction); 'so shall it be easier for thyself, and they shall bear the burden with thee' (the burden shared, eased for Moses, borne by the judges with him, the wisdom of delegation, the load divided, Numbers 11:17, the Spirit on the seventy, the providence, the relief of Moses, the better government) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And let them judge the people at all seasons: and it shall be, that every great matter they shall bring unto thee, but every small matter they shall judge: so shall it be easier for thyself, and they shall bear the burden with thee
the sharing of the burden among the judges
the wisdom of delegation, the load divided (Numbers 11:17)
the relief of Moses, the better government

schools.summary: Jethro's plan submitted to God's command: 'if thou shalt do this thing, and God command thee so, then thou shalt be able to endure, and all this people shall also go to their place in peace' — Jethro submitting his counsel to God's command ('and God command thee so') — the plan to be followed only if God approved it (the humble deference, the counsel subject to God's will, not imposed, the wise modesty, Jethro not presuming above God); Jethro's deference ('if God command thee so') — the counsel subject to God's approval (the wisdom of submitting the plan to God, the providence, God indeed approving, Numbers 11:16-17; Deuteronomy 1:9-18, the humble counsel); 'then thou shalt be able to endure, and all this people shall also go to their place in peace' (the double benefit, Moses able to endure, not worn out, v.18, the people going home in peace, their causes settled without delay, the wisdom of the plan, the relief of Moses, the satisfaction of the people, the providence, the ordering of the government for the good of all) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: If thou shalt do this thing, and God command thee so, then thou shalt be able to endure, and all this people shall also go to their place in peace
Jethro's plan submitted to God's command
the humble deference - the counsel subject to God's will (Deuteronomy 1:9-18)
the double benefit - the relief of Moses, the peace of the people

schools.summary: Moses humbly receiving Jethro's counsel: 'so Moses hearkened to the voice of his father in law, and did all that he had said' — Moses, the great leader, humbly receiving the counsel of his father-in-law and doing all he advised (the meekness of Moses, Numbers 12:3, 'the man Moses was very meek' — the great prophet learning from the Gentile priest, the wisdom of receiving good counsel, whoever the counsellor, the humility of the leader); Moses' hearkening (humbly receiving the advice) — the meekness (the great man not despising the counsel of his inferior, the lesson, the wisdom of receiving good counsel, even from the lowly, Proverbs 12:15, 'he that hearkeneth unto counsel is wise'); 'and did all that he had said' (Moses doing all the counsel, the full compliance, the wisdom in practice, the plan carried out, vv.25-26, the providence, the good counsel followed, the government ordered, God confirming the plan, Numbers 11:16-17; Deuteronomy 1:9-18) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

themes: So Moses hearkened to the voice of his father in law, and did all that he had said
Moses humbly receiving Jethro's counsel
the meekness of the great leader (Numbers 12:3)
the wisdom of receiving good counsel, even from the lowly (Proverbs 12:15)

schools.summary: Moses appointing the rulers as counseled: 'and Moses chose able men out of all Israel, and made them heads over the people, rulers of thousands, rulers of hundreds, rulers of fifties, and rulers of tens' — Moses choosing able men (the qualified, v.21) and setting them as rulers over the graded divisions (thousands, hundreds, fifties, tens, the carrying-out of the counsel, the plan put into effect, the ordering of the government, the judges appointed, the burden shared, the wisdom in practice); Moses choosing the able men (the qualified, v.21, the choice ratified by the people, Deuteronomy 1:13) — the appointment of the judges; 'rulers of thousands, rulers of hundreds, rulers of fifties, and rulers of tens' (the graded order, the hierarchy of judges, vv.21-22, the orderly government, the providence, the burden shared, the relief of Moses, the better order) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Moses chose able men out of all Israel, and made them heads over the people, rulers of thousands, rulers of hundreds, rulers of fifties, and rulers of tens
Moses appointing the rulers as counseled
the plan carried out, the judges appointed (v.21; Deuteronomy 1:13)
the burden shared, the government ordered

schools.summary: The judges judging while Moses kept the hard cases: 'and they judged the people at all seasons: the hard causes they brought unto Moses, but every small matter they judged themselves' — the appointed judges judging the people at all times, bringing the hard cases to Moses and deciding the small matters themselves (the order working, the plan in effect, vv.22, the shared burden, the lesser cases to the judges, the harder to Moses, the relief of Moses); the hard cases to Moses (the great matters reserved for the chief, vv.22) — the graded jurisdiction (the order maintained); 'every small matter they judged themselves' (the small cases decided by the judges, the burden shared, the relief of Moses, the load divided, vv.22, the providence, the orderly government, the wisdom of the plan working) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And they judged the people at all seasons: the hard causes they brought unto Moses, but every small matter they judged themselves
the judges judging while Moses kept the hard cases
the plan working, the graded jurisdiction (v.22)
the burden shared, the orderly government

schools.summary: Jethro departing to his own land: 'and Moses let his father in law depart; and he went his way into his own land' — Moses sending Jethro away (letting him depart), and Jethro returning to his own land (Midian, the parting, after the visit, the worship, the counsel, the return of the Gentile priest, Numbers 10:29-32, the later invitation, the Kenites, his descendants, dwelling with Israel, Judges 1:16, the close of the visit); Moses sending Jethro away (with honor, after the visit) — the parting (the close of the fellowship); 'he went his way into his own land' (Jethro returning to Midian, his own land, the providence, the Gentile priest who had worshipped and counseled now departing, the Kenites later joined to Israel, Judges 1:16; 1 Samuel 15:6, the kindness to the descendants of Jethro, the close of the episode before the giving of the law, ch. 19) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And Moses let his father in law depart; and he went his way into his own land
Jethro departing to his own land
the honorable parting after the visit, worship, and counsel
the Kenites, his descendants, later joined to Israel (Judges 1:16); before the giving of the law (ch. 19)
